outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2792:      "content": "The carbon-fluorine bond imparts thermodynamic and kinetic stability that prevents hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial transformation pathways typical of conventional organic contaminants [3]. Furthermore, this structural integrity ensures that PFAS remain chemically intact in subsurface environments rather than degrading into benign products [4]. Furthermore, the classification of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" reflects this exceptional resistance to degradation across environmental matrices [3].\n\nChain-length differentiation governs how persistence manifests in aquifer systems, as detailed in the examination of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate substantially reduced interfacial accumulation [5], remaining predominantly aqueous-phase and facilitating more rapid transport to saturated zones—a mobility pattern addressed in the analysis of Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones. Nevertheless, the persistence characteristic thus translates into divergent contamination architectures: long-chain compounds establish retarded but enduring source zones in vadose soils, while short-chain analogs generate rapidly disseminating plumes with limited formation of retrievable contaminant masses [3].\n\nThe relationship between soil-phase and aqueous-phase concentrations directly governs leaching potential to underlying groundwater [6]. However, long-chain compounds, with their stronger sorptive affinity and interfacial accumulation, exhibit extended residence times in unsaturated soils before groundwater impact—a temporal delay explored in Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones, yet their equivalent chemical stability ensures decades to centuries of aquifer contamination once mobilized [4]. Short-chain persistence combined with higher mobility produces faster aquifer penetration with equally protracted contamination duration [4].\n\nLong-chain PFAS demonstrate pronounced bioaccumulation in aquatic organisms, effectively transferring from water column to biological tissues and potentially delaying groundwater recovery even after source removal [4]. Moreover, this biological persistence creates an additional compartment for contaminant storage and subsequent release. Short-chain PFAS, while exhibiting reduced bioconcentration factors, maintain sufficient environmental persistence to ensure continuous water column availability [3].\n\nNatural attenuation proves nonviable as a management strategy for either chain-length category. Moreover, unlike petroleum hydrocarbons or chlorinated solvents where transformation products may exhibit reduced toxicity or mobility, PFAS degradation—when it occurs—produces terminal perfluorinated acids that retain environmental persistence and often equivalent or greater mobility than parent compounds. This absence of attenuation products suitable for natural recovery forces reliance on engineered removal systems. In contrast, conventional pump-and-treat remediation assumes contaminant mass depletion over operational timeframes, yet PFAS persistence ensures that extracted water concentrations remain stable absent physical removal mechanisms. Treatment system operation must extend to decadal scales rather than the years typical for biodegradable contaminants.\n\nThe stability characteristics redefine aquifer vulnerability assessment by eliminating recovery timelines and natural attenuation capacity from decision frameworks. However, the differential expression of persistence between chain lengths—mobility-versus-retention trade-offs—determines specific aquifer risk profiles but not the fundamental requirement for permanent institutional controls. These persistence properties interact with transport mechanisms to produce contamination patterns that distinguish PFAS from all other groundwater pollutant classes.\n\nThe thermodynamic stability of PFAS compounds derives fundamentally from the carbon-fluorine bond dissociation energy, approximately 485 kJ/mol, which exceeds the carbon-hydrogen bond energy by roughly 35%. In addition, this bond strength renders hydrolytic cleavage kinetically inaccessible under ambient aquifer conditions spanning typical pH ranges of 6.5–8.5 and temperatures of 10–15°C in temperate groundwater systems [6]. Nevertheless, neither abiotic nor biotic transformation pathways achieve measurable degradation rates for perfluorinated structures, distinguishing PFAS from virtually all other organic contaminant classes where natural attenuation contributes to mass reduction.\n\nThe \"forever chemical\" designation carries specific regulatory and operational implications for aquifer management that extend beyond colloquial usage. PFAS do not readily degrade in nature, a characteristic that eliminates time-dependent remediation strategies from consideration. Additionally, this stability and persistence in the environment ensures that contamination episodes effectively represent permanent alterations to aquifer geochemistry. The accumulation potential compounds this concern, as continuous low-concentration inputs from diffuse sources generate ever-increasing mass burdens without compensatory loss mechanisms.\n\nChain-length modulates how persistence expresses spatially within aquifer systems. In contrast, long-chain PFAS (C≥7 perfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids and C≥6 perfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids) exhibit stronger hydrophobic character and fluorophilic interactions with soil organic matter, producing retardation factors that delay groundwater arrival but extend source zone longevity. Short-chain analogs demonstrate reduced sorptive affinity, translating persistence into more rapid plume development. However, both categories maintain sufficient stability that transport velocity variations do not affect ultimate contamination duration—decades to centuries characterize either scenario once aquifer penetration occurs.\n\nThe persistence properties fundamentally constrain remediation technology selection. Additionally, engineered systems must assume indefinite operation without the depletion curves that justify treatment endpoint decisions for biodegradable contaminants. Advanced oxidation, reduction, or biological treatment processes applicable to other organic pollutants prove ineffective against perfluorinated structures, requiring energy-intensive separation technologies such as granular activated carbon, ion exchange, or membrane filtration that concentrate rather than destroy contaminant mass. The stability that prevents environmental degradation simultaneously prevents treatment degradation, creating permanent waste management obligations for spent treatment media.\n\nAquifer vulnerability assessment frameworks require recalibration to accommodate these persistence characteristics. Nevertheless, traditional risk assessment methodologies incorporate contaminant half-lives when predicting future concentration distributions; for PFAS, effectively infinite persistence eliminates this temporal dimension from predictive models. In addition, source zone identification and characterization assume heightened importance given that removal represents the only mechanism for aquifer protection. The differential mobility between chain lengths determines plume geometry and extraction well spacing, yet the shared persistence property ensures that any mass reaching groundwater constitutes a permanent liability requiring active management in perpetuity or until technological breakthroughs achieve defluorination under ambient conditions.",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2821:      "content": "PFAS contamination spans water, air, soil, fish, wildlife, and human populations across national boundaries, establishing the environmental ubiquity that enables differential leaching behaviors between chain-length categories. In contrast, this pervasive distribution creates interconnected pathways through which surface-released compounds migrate toward subsurface aquifer systems. In addition, research examining unsaturated zone mass transfer reveals that soil-phase measurements alone inadequately predict leaching risk; rather, the concentration gradient between retained and mobile fractions determines vertical transport kinetics [6]. This partitioning behavior varies systematically with perfluorinated chain length. Field quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water addresses a critical literature gap in understanding compartmental exchange dynamics [7]. Moreover, bidirectional flux occurs near manufacturing facilities, with contaminated groundwater discharging to surface water while surface infiltration simultaneously recharges aquifers [7]. This hydrological connectivity ensures that surface water contamination propagates through river networks and reservoir infrastructure into underlying groundwater reserves. In particular, long-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS accumulate substantially at air-water interfaces, exceeding the interfacial affinity of short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA [5]. This differential interfacial partitioning has direct implications for subsurface transport: stronger air-water interfacial adsorption retards long-chain compound mobility through unsaturated soils, while short-chain compounds with diminished interfacial affinity migrate more rapidly through vadose zones [5]. The measured and simulated data confirm that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil matrices [8]. Additionally, groundwater concentration ranges demonstrate the practical outcome of these transport dynamics. Total PFAS concentrations in natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film-forming foam reached approximately 15,000 ng L−1, indicating substantial subsurface migration capacity despite chain-length-dependent retention mechanisms [5]. Additionally, in urbanized and agricultural settings, groundwater samples exhibited total PFAS concentrations varying between 22 and 718 ng L−1, confirming widespread aquifer contamination across diverse land-use contexts [9]. The classification framework distinguishing short-chain from long-chain PFAS derives from bioaccumulation potential rather than transport behavior alone. This regulatory distinction carries environmental partitioning implications: compounds with elevated bioaccumulation factors typically exhibit stronger hydrophobic and lipophilic characteristics that enhance soil retention while potentially delaying groundwater arrival. Additionally, short-chain replacements with reduced bioaccumulation potential demonstrate correspondingly diminished subsurface attenuation. Atmospheric transport extends contamination beyond point-source footprints, enabling precipitation-recharge area contamination distant from industrial or firefighting application sites. However, this aerial dispersion mechanism creates geographically distributed source areas that complicate aquifer protection strategies, particularly for short-chain compounds with elevated vapor pressures. The atmospheric pathway's contribution to background contamination levels challenges conventional source-control approaches. Soil profiles function as critical transition zones where differential retention between chain-length categories manifests. However, long-chain compounds exhibit stronger sorption in surface soils and sediments, creating delayed but persistent groundwater contamination potential [8]. Short-chain compounds demonstrate reduced sorption affinity, facilitating more rapid vertical migration through unsaturated zones. Additionally, these contrasting temporal pathways yield distinct concentration profiles in receiving aquifers. The nationwide distribution patterns establish that aquifer vulnerability assessment must transcend source identification to emphasize transport pathway analysis and compound-specific mobility prediction. Contamination ubiquity means both long-chain and short-chain PFAS possess demonstrated capacity to reach groundwater systems, but through mechanistically distinct routes that govern exposure timing and magnitude.\n\nThe environmental compartments through which PFAS migrate exhibit distinct retention capacities that systematically favor short-chain compound propagation toward aquifer systems. Additionally, surface soil horizons demonstrate particularly pronounced discrimination between chain-length categories, with organic carbon content and mineral surface chemistry creating compound-specific sorption isotherms [8]. In addition, this subsurface selectivity explains why groundwater monitoring networks detect short-chain PFAS at greater frequencies and concentrations relative to their emission proportions, indicating preferential vertical transport through the vadose zone.\n\nThe temporal dimension of aquifer contamination reveals asymmetric arrival patterns between compound classes. Long-chain PFAS exhibit delayed but sustained groundwater fluxes, creating contamination plumes with extended duration but reduced peak concentrations [8]. In addition, short-chain analogs generate sharper concentration fronts that reach monitoring wells earlier, potentially preceding regulatory detection thresholds and response protocols. This temporal decoupling complicates risk assessment frameworks that assume synchronous transport across the PFAS structural spectrum.\n\nPrecipitation chemistry fundamentally influences leaching potential through its effects on ionic strength and dissolved organic matter mobilization. Furthermore, rainfall events trigger episodic flushing of soil-adsorbed PFAS, with the magnitude of pulse releases correlating inversely with perfluorinated chain length. Short-chain compounds demonstrate greater hydrologic responsiveness, exhibiting concentration spikes in shallow groundwater following storm events that minimally perturb long-chain compound profiles. Furthermore, this differential reactivity suggests that climate-driven intensification of precipitation patterns will disproportionately accelerate short-chain PFAS aquifer contamination.\n\nThe vadose zone thickness emerges as a critical mediator of chain-length-dependent attenuation. Additionally, thicker unsaturated profiles provide extended residence times where sorption-desorption kinetics and biodegradation potentials differentially affect compound classes [6]. Thin vadose zones or fractured bedrock settings minimize these attenuation opportunities, enabling rapid transmission of both short-chain and long-chain PFAS to underlying aquifers. This geologic context dependency explains the spatial heterogeneity observed in groundwater contamination severity across otherwise similar land-use settings.\n\nAquifer geochemistry further modulates post-arrival PFAS behavior, with redox conditions and mineral surface charges influencing persistence and mobility within saturated zones [7]. Consequently, anoxic groundwater conditions may enhance long-chain PFAS stability through diminished oxidative transformation pathways, while oxic settings potentially favor short-chain compound persistence due to their relative recalcitrance to biodegradation under aerobic conditions. Moreover, these subsurface biogeochemical interactions remain understudied relative to vadose zone transport processes, representing a critical knowledge gap for predictive groundwater quality modeling.\n\nThe nationwide distribution of PFAS manufacturing and application facilities creates source-area legacies that continue driving leaching decades after active use cessation. Historical aqueous film-forming foam application sites function as persistent contamination archives, with soil reservoirs releasing PFAS through gradual desorption and dissolution processes. Furthermore, this legacy dimension ensures that aquifer contamination will persist as a management challenge irrespective of current emission controls, necessitating long-term monitoring strategies calibrated to compound-specific transport velocities.",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2852:      "content": "Air-water interfacial partitioning establishes a primary mechanism by which chain length governs PFAS transport through unsaturated zones. Moreover, this differential accumulation arises from amplified amphiphilic character with chain extension—longer fluorocarbon tails increasingly orient toward non-aqueous phases while charged head groups remain water-anchored, creating thermodynamically favorable conditions for interface residence. However, the environmental significance of this interfacial accumulation extends to soil distribution patterns. Soil pores contain extensive interfacial area, particularly under intermediate moisture conditions, providing substantial retention capacity that operates independently of mineral surface sorption or organic matter partitioning. This mechanism represents an additional partitioning pathway that short-chain compounds largely cannot exploit. Moreover, long-chain PFAS do not merely experience transport retardation; they partition into distinct physical domains that fundamentally alter migration pathways. The air-water interface functions as a temporary reservoir that buffers long-chain compounds against rapid leaching while simultaneously generating concentration gradients that drive lateral diffusion and non-equilibrium transport behaviors. However, long-chain PFAS exhibit pronounced depth stratification in soil profiles, with accumulation near source zones and diminished penetration to groundwater, whereas short-chain homologues demonstrate more uniform vertical distribution extending deeper into the profile —a pattern with direct implications for groundwater concentration baselines discussed in Section s07 (Groundwater Concentration Baselines and Background Levels). This divergence creates a fundamental asymmetry in aquifer contamination potential. Chain length inversely correlates with interfacial accumulation but directly correlates with environmental persistence, establishing opposing controls on leaching behavior. Furthermore, the interfacial accumulation effect competes with dissolution-driven transport, sequestering long-chain compounds in stationary phases that resist advective mobilization. Short-chain PFAS, lacking comparable interfacial affinity, remain preferentially dissolved in porewater and thus more susceptible to vertical transport through gravitational drainage and capillary-driven flow. Moreover, foam fractionation selectively exploits this interfacial accumulation behavior for long-chain PFAS removal, with CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrating capacity to reduce total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below 8 ng L−1 detection limits within 15 minutes. This mechanistic linkage—detailed in Section s05 (Surface Activity and Solubility Controls on Foam Fractionation and Solution Retention)—indicates that remediation approaches effective for long-chain PFAS removal from water simultaneously reveal where these compounds accumulate in subsurface environments. Weak interfacial accumulation of short-chain PFAS limits both their foam fractionation recoverability and their natural attenuation through vadose zone retention. Additionally, transport models that incorporate only equilibrium sorption to solid phases systematically underestimate long-chain PFAS retention and overestimate their groundwater penetration rates. Inclusion of air-water interfacial partitioning as a distinct mass transfer mechanism substantially improves predictive accuracy for vadose zone transport scenarios. In addition, this refinement carries practical significance for risk assessment, as conventional modeling approaches may erroneously prioritize long-chain compounds as primary leaching threats when short-chain alternatives actually dominate groundwater fluxes. The temporal dimension of differential interfacial accumulation structures aquifer contamination evolution over multi-decadal timescales. Long-chain PFAS retained through interfacial and solid-phase partitioning constitute secondary sources—analogous to sediment contamination hotspots examined in Section s06 (Sediment Contamination Hotspots)—subject to gradual release through changing moisture conditions, chemical gradients, or biological perturbation, whereas short-chain compounds passing through vadose zones establish more immediate groundwater contamination. In addition, this selectivity has generated an environmental legacy wherein groundwater contamination increasingly comprises short-chain homologues that have replaced regulated long-chain precursors in industrial applications. The substitution pattern has produced aquifer systems with contaminant profiles skewed toward compounds exhibiting minimal natural attenuation capacity through interfacial retention, necessitating monitoring strategies that account for both present short-chain dominance and future long-chain legacy contributions. Additionally, the quantitative disparity in interfacial accumulation between chain length classes carries profound implications for transport modeling parameterization. This differential manifests in surface excess concentrations that can exceed those of short-chain homologues by orders of magnitude, fundamentally altering the partitioning behavior that governs subsurface migration. The magnitude of this accumulation disparity directly determines the retardation factor applicable to vadose zone transport simulations—models that fail to capture this chain-length-dependent interfacial affinity will systematically misrepresent plume arrival times and concentration profiles. Furthermore, the interfacial accumulation mechanism operates synergistically with other retention processes to create compound-specific transport signatures. While solid-phase sorption to organic matter and mineral surfaces provides baseline retardation for all PFAS classes, air-water interfacial partitioning introduces an additional retention dimension that scales non-linearly with fluorocarbon chain length. Moreover, this scaling relationship means that incremental chain extension produces disproportionate gains in interfacial residence time, generating sharp transport contrasts between adjacent homologues in chromatographic separation terms. Short-chain PFAS, positioned below the threshold where amphiphilic character drives substantial interfacial affinity, behave as conservative solutes in unsaturated zone hydrology, advancing at velocities approaching those of the mobile water phase. The environmental persistence of long-chain PFAS compounds—compounds that resist hydrolysis, photolysis, and microbial degradation—couples with their interfacial accumulation to create sustained secondary source zones. In addition, these reservoirs retain contaminant mass for decades following initial release, functioning as time-delayed inputs to groundwater systems rather than instantaneous pulses. The temporal structure of this release fundamentally shapes aquifer contamination evolution, with long-chain compounds contributing delayed but prolonged contamination episodes that may emerge only after short-chain plumes have already traversed the system. In contrast, this temporal phasing complicates source attribution efforts and demands monitoring programs capable of detecting contamination signals across extended timescales. The practical significance of differential interfacial accumulation extends to treatment technology selection and performance prediction. Foam fractionation systems exploit the same physicochemical principle that governs natural subsurface retention—selective enrichment of long-chain PFAS at air-water interfaces—to achieve removal efficiencies that reflect compound-specific surface activity. Furthermore, systems optimized for long-chain PFAS capture demonstrate limited effectiveness against short-chain contamination, mirroring the natural attenuation limitations observed in vadose zone environments. This parallel between engineered and natural systems underscores the fundamental control exerted by molecular structure on environmental fate, reinforcing the necessity of chain-length-specific approaches in both remediation design and risk assessment frameworks. Moreover, the substitution of long-chain PFAS with short-chain alternatives in industrial formulations, driven by regulatory pressures on PFOA and PFOS, has inadvertently shifted aquifer contamination profiles toward compounds that circumvent natural interfacial retention barriers. This transition has produced groundwater systems increasingly dominated by mobile, poorly attenuated contaminants that resist both natural attenuation and conventional treatment technologies. The environmental legacy of this substitution pattern thus comprises not merely a change in contaminant identity but a fundamental alteration in aquifer vulnerability—systems previously buffered by interfacial retention capacity now face contamination threats characterized by minimal subsurface attenuation and maximal transport efficiency. Additionally, the classification framework distinguishing short- and long-chain PFAS rests fundamentally upon bioaccumulation potential rather than transport behavior, establishing criteria that misalign with groundwater vulnerability assessment priorities. This definitional basis carries significant implications for regulatory frameworks, as compounds classified by ecological hazard may exhibit markedly divergent subsurface mobility characteristics. In contrast, the resulting regulatory blind spot permits short-chain alternatives to circumvent restrictions despite their enhanced aquifer penetration capacity. Empirical investigations of AFFF-impacted sites demonstrate that relationships between soil-phase and porewater-phase PFAS concentrations govern leaching potential to underlying groundwater systems. These measurements reveal that interfacial accumulation mechanisms decouple soil concentration from leaching risk—high soil burdens of long-chain compounds may indicate effective retention rather than imminent groundwater threat. As a result, modest soil concentrations of short-chain PFAS can mask substantial groundwater contamination potential due to their preferential partitioning into mobile porewater. Field-scale quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water remains underdeveloped in the environmental literature, representing a critical knowledge gap for watershed-scale contamination assessment [7]. In addition, this deficiency particularly affects understanding of how vadose zone interfacial retention ultimately influences groundwater discharge dynamics and surface water loading. The absence of field-validated mass transfer coefficients limits predictive capacity for contaminant flux estimation across hydrologic boundaries. This concentration range reflects composite contributions from multiple chain-length homologues, with short-chain compounds typically dominating dissolved-phase profiles due to their limited interfacial retention. Furthermore, the upper bound of this range approaches the treatment demonstration concentrations where foam fractionation achieves near-complete removal, suggesting that engineered systems must accommodate realistic contamination scenarios encountered in field settings. This persistence couples with chain-length-dependent interfacial accumulation to create compound-specific residence time distributions in the vadose zone. In addition, long-chain PFAS resist both chemical transformation and physical mobilization, establishing secondary source zones with decade-scale longevity. Short-chain alternatives share persistence characteristics but lack complementary retention mechanisms, enabling rapid transit through subsurface systems that compounds their effective environmental mobility. The quantification of air-water interfacial accumulation reveals substantial disparities between chain-length classes. Moreover, this differential accumulation establishes a physical basis for chromatographic separation in natural systems, whereby longer fluorocarbon tails generate higher surface excess concentrations that translate directly into enhanced retention capacity. The magnitude of this disparity fundamentally alters mass transfer kinetics between stationary and mobile phases in unsaturated porous media. Moreover, these measurements reveal that interfacial accumulation mechanisms decouple total soil concentration from imminent leaching risk—substantial soil burdens of long-chain compounds may indicate effective vadose zone retention rather than impending groundwater contamination. Modest soil concentrations of short-chain PFAS can obscure significant groundwater contamination potential due to their preferential partitioning into mobile porewater fractions. This definitional basis creates regulatory blind spots wherein compounds classified by ecological hazard criteria may exhibit markedly divergent subsurface mobility characteristics. Furthermore, short-chain alternatives that circumvent bioaccumulation-based restrictions can simultaneously demonstrate enhanced aquifer penetration capacity, producing a misalignment between regulatory priorities and groundwater vulnerability assessment needs. Foam fractionation technology exploits differential interfacial accumulation for engineered removal. In addition, CTAB addition to foam fractionation systems achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters. However, the underlying physicochemical selectivity persists—systems optimized for long-chain capture remain less effective against short-chain contamination, mirroring the natural attenuation limitations observed in vadose zone environments. outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2864:      "content": "Long-chain PFAS exhibit substantially greater accumulation at air-water interfaces than short-chain congeners, establishing fundamental boundary conditions for foam-based separation efficacy in aquifer remediation. In addition, this differential interfacial partitioning directly determines susceptibility to foam fractionation, a physical separation technology increasingly deployed for PFAS-impacted groundwater. Furthermore, foam fractionation exploits the tendency of surfactant-like molecules to concentrate at air-water interfaces and become entrained within rising foam bubbles. The molecular architecture underlying this disparity involves increased fluorinated carbon chain length amplifying hydrophobic character while maintaining hydrophilic functional groups, creating the amphiphilic structure essential for interfacial partitioning. Short-chain PFAS, with truncated fluorinated tails, exhibit diminished hydrophobicity and reduced propensity for air-water interface accumulation. Additionally, this differential surface activity generates pronounced treatment efficacy disparities. Short-chain PFAS demonstrate incomplete removal despite extended treatment durations, their limited surface activity preventing effective enrichment within foam phases. Furthermore, groundwater systems contaminated with short-chain PFAS or mixed chain-length assemblages typical of industrial precursor degradation experience incomplete treatment through foam fractionation alone. The physicochemical drivers underlying this differential behavior extend beyond simple hydrophobicity metrics. Solution retention characteristics—the propensity for PFAS to remain dissolved rather than partition into separable phases—depend upon complex interactions between molecular structure, water chemistry, and interfacial dynamics. Additionally, long-chain PFAS readily depart bulk solution through foam entrainment, whereas short-chain PFAS resist such partitioning, maintaining higher aqueous concentrations and exhibiting enhanced mobility within subsurface systems (see also: Soil-Porewater Partitioning Mechanisms in Unsaturated Zones). These observations illuminate fundamental constraints on foam-based remediation strategies. Additionally, technologies optimized for long-chain PFAS removal underperform when applied to short-chain-dominated contamination scenarios. The solution retention exhibited by short-chain compounds ensures their continued presence in groundwater, facilitating transport through porous media and expanding contaminant plumes. Treatment system design must account for this chain-length-dependent fractionation susceptibility, potentially necessitating sequential or hybrid approaches addressing both compound classes. Moreover, the connection between surface activity and leaching potential operates through multiple pathways. Reduced foam fractionation efficiency for short-chain PFAS implies these compounds persist longer in aqueous phases, sustaining driving gradients for vertical migration through unsaturated zones (see also: Nationwide Environmental Distribution Patterns Across Chain Lengths). Furthermore, long-chain PFAS removed through interfacial processes undergo reconcentration in foamate, requiring subsequent treatment or disposal that introduces secondary management challenges. The differential solution retention characteristics thus shape not only immediate treatment outcomes but also long-term contaminant fate and transport trajectories within aquifer systems. Gas-to-liquid ratios and bubble size distributions, critical determinants of interfacial surface area generation, exhibit diminished returns for short-chain compounds despite optimization for long-chain removal. Additionally, this technological limitation stems from the fundamental thermodynamic disadvantage that short-chain PFAS face at air-water interfaces, where reduced van der Waals interactions with the gaseous phase limit their competitive adsorption against other surface-active species. The persistence of short-chain PFAS in treated effluent necessitates extended hydraulic retention times or multiple treatment stages, substantially increasing operational costs and energy demands relative to long-chain-dominated contamination scenarios. Moreover, the implications for aquifer vulnerability assessment are substantial. Groundwater monitoring programs relying upon foam fractionation as a primary remediation technology risk systematically underestimating short-chain PFAS plume advancement due to treatment inefficiency masquerading as concentration reduction. This analytical artifact—where apparent remediation success reflects technological incapacity rather than genuine contaminant elimination—creates hazardous blind spots in environmental management strategies. Moreover, short-chain PFAS continuing through treatment barriers maintain their leaching potential, penetrating deeper into saturated zones where subsequent recovery becomes progressively more challenging and expensive. Comparative analysis of chain-length-specific behavior illuminates why precursor transformation products increasingly dominate environmental detection profiles. In addition, as industrial formulations shift toward shorter-chain alternatives and atmospheric or biotic degradation progressively truncates longer congeners, the resulting mixture composition evolves toward compounds inherently resistant to interfacial separation technologies. This compositional trajectory toward remediation-recalcitrant species represents a fundamental challenge for groundwater protection strategies predicated upon foam-based treatment. The temporal dimension of this transformation—operating across decades in subsurface environments—ensures that current contamination profiles will progressively shift toward short-chain dominance, degrading treatment system performance even where initial design specifications appeared adequate. Moreover, the reconcentration dynamics of foamate management introduce secondary contamination risks that differentially affect long-chain versus short-chain PFAS fate. Long-chain compounds successfully entrained within foam phases require subsequent destruction or secure disposal, with thermal treatment or deep well injection representing common management pathways. However, the substantial volume reduction achieved through foam fractionation—typically 10-fold to 100-fold concentration factors—simultaneously generates waste streams with elevated toxicity and regulatory classification challenges. For short-chain PFAS, the inverse problem emerges: their residence within treated groundwater sustains exposure pathways while evading capture in manageable waste volumes. This asymmetry in contaminant partitioning between aqueous and foam phases thus bifurcates environmental risk profiles, with long-chain PFAS posing concentrated disposal challenges and short-chain compounds presenting diffuse, persistent aquifer contamination. Moreover, the efficacy of cationic surfactant enhancement reveals critical nuances in addressing chain-length-dependent treatment limitations. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) addition achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters, with total concentrations in AFFF-impacted natural groundwaters reduced from approximately 15,000 ng L−1 to below 8 ng L−1 detection limits within 15 minutes. However, this technological breakthrough does not eliminate fundamental thermodynamic constraints; rather, it introduces auxiliary surface-active agents that competitively partition to air-water interfaces, potentially co-entraining short-chain PFAS through indirect mechanisms rather than direct interfacial adsorption. Six cationic surfactants have been systematically evaluated for this dual-chain-length removal capability, indicating ongoing optimization efforts across the surfactant class. This definitional foundation—established prior to widespread recognition of differential interfacial activity—means that compounds sharing regulatory categorization may exhibit vastly disparate foam fractionation susceptibilities. Additionally, treatment systems designed around chain-length classifications optimized for ecological risk assessment may inadequately capture transport-relevant physicochemical gradients. Field-scale groundwater contamination magnifies these technological constraints. Moreover, total PFAS concentrations in groundwater samples from urbanized and agricultural regions vary between 22 and 718 ng L−1, spanning concentration ranges where foam fractionation efficacy transitions from quantitative removal to fractional elimination [9]. At lower concentrations approaching the lower bound of this range, interfacial competition effects become proportionally more pronounced, disproportionately compromising short-chain PFAS removal where surface activity deficits already limit enrichment efficiency. The soil-porewater partitioning dynamics underlying leaching potential demonstrate pronounced chain-length dependence through air-water interfacial adsorption mechanisms. Moreover, this interfacially-mediated retention constitutes a double-edged phenomenon: while it retards vertical migration of long-chain compounds through unsaturated zones, it simultaneously depletes their availability for foam-based extraction once groundwater saturation is reached. Short-chain PFAS, exhibit diminished interfacial retention in soils yet correspondingly elevated mobility toward groundwater tables—precisely the compounds most resistant to subsequent foam fractionation recovery. Furthermore, the research trajectory connecting unsaturated soil concentrations to mobile porewater fractions capable of groundwater migration remains underdeveloped, representing critical uncertainty in predictive vulnerability assessment. Mass transfer quantification between groundwater and surface water systems—essential for understanding complete hydrologic cycle contamination—has historically relied upon modeling rather than empirical field measurement, though recent studies have begun addressing this data gap. The \"forever chemical\" designation, reflecting recalcitrance to natural degradation processes, ensures that chain-length transformation and differential mobility effects persist across decadal timescales without attenuation through biochemical pathways. Furthermore, the regulatory classification framework governing PFAS compounds predates contemporary understanding of differential interfacial partitioning behavior, creating structural misalignment between ecological risk categories and transport-relevant physicochemical properties. This definitional foundation—established prior to widespread recognition of differential interfacial activity—means that compounds sharing regulatory categorization may exhibit vastly disparate foam fractionation susceptibilities. Additionally, treatment systems designed around chain-length classifications optimized for ecological risk assessment may inadequately capture transport-relevant physicochemical gradients. In addition, these quantitative benchmarks enable assessment of whether observed concentrations reflect point-source releases, diffuse regional inputs, or natural attenuation processes. Nevertheless, this spread reflects heterogeneous source distributions, variable transport distances, and differential attenuation processes. The upper bound of this range substantially exceeds typical regulatory screening levels for individual PFAS compounds, indicating that cumulative exposures in affected aquifers present environmental health concerns even when single-analyte concentrations appear modest. The analytical significance of these concentration ranges extends beyond magnitude comparisons. Furthermore, groundwater systems function as integrative reservoirs where PFAS loading reflects time-integrated source inputs modulated by subsurface transport dynamics. Long-chain PFAS exhibit stronger hydrophobic interactions and sorptive affinity to solid phases, theoretically producing attenuated transport distances and elevated concentrations near source zones. However, short-chain homologs possess greater mobility potential, suggesting broader spatial distribution at potentially lower peak concentrations—though aggregated total PFAS measurements limit direct mechanistic interpretation. Establishing true background contamination levels presents methodological challenges. The ubiquity of PFAS in contemporary environmental systems, including atmospheric deposition and agricultural amendments, complicates discrimination between natural background and diffuse anthropogenic inputs. In addition, the lower concentration bound of 22 ng L⁻¹ likely represents a practical detection limit for composite PFAS analysis rather than a genuine geochemical baseline, indicating that even minimally impacted groundwater systems exhibit measurable PFAS burdens. The distinction between point-source contamination plumes and regional background elevation carries profound implications for leaching potential assessment. Furthermore, point-source scenarios, typical of industrial facilities and firefighting training areas, generate steep concentration gradients where long-chain PFAS accumulate near source zones, while short-chain compounds penetrate deeper into aquifer systems. This differential penetration creates characteristic concentration profiles that invert with transport distance—long-chain species declining precipitously while short-chain concentrations persist, ultimately establishing mobile contamination fronts dominated by short-chain homologs. Regional background elevation results from atmospheric transport and deposition coupled with agricultural PFAS applications, producing more uniform concentration distributions where chain-length profiles reflect source signatures rather than transport fractionation. In addition, the 22–718 ng L⁻¹ range encompasses both contamination regimes, with higher values indicating proximity to primary sources and lower values representing diluted or aged contamination. This mechanistic understanding explains why groundwater contamination profiles frequently show short-chain PFAS dominance at distance from source zones. In addition, treatment implications of these concentration baselines are substantial. The temporal dimension of groundwater contamination introduces critical complexity to baseline interpretation. Furthermore, concentration ranges measured at single time points cannot capture the dynamic evolution of PFAS plumes, where source zone depletion, aquifer recharge fluctuations, and seasonal water table variations produce substantial concentration variability. This temporal instability complicates efforts to establish stable background thresholds, as concentrations previously interpreted as baseline may instead represent transient states within evolving contamination scenarios. In addition, the analytical methods employed for PFAS quantification directly influence perceived baseline levels. Detection limits for individual compounds vary substantially across analytical platforms, with some short-chain homologs exhibiting poorer sensitivity than their long-chain counterparts due to ionization inefficiencies in mass spectrometric detection. Dissolved organic carbon competes with PFAS for sorption sites or facilitates co-transport through formation of mobile complexes, particularly affecting long-chain compounds with stronger hydrophobic character. Moreover, these geochemical controls generate site-specific concentration baselines that resist universal application, necessitating aquifer-specific characterization for accurate leaching potential assessment. The Brazilian study context reveals important geographic variability in baseline establishment. Urbanized and agricultural settings present distinct source portfolios compared to industrial point sources, with atmospheric deposition and legacy agricultural PFAS applications contributing to diffuse background elevation. Additionally, this source heterogeneity means that concentration ranges established in one hydrogeologic setting may misrepresent baseline conditions elsewhere, particularly where industrial PFAS manufacturing or intensive aqueous film-forming foam usage has occurred. This mechanistic insight explains observed discrepancies between regional soil PFAS inventories and corresponding groundwater baselines. outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:2916:      "content": "Additionally, this differential surface activity originates from the pronounced hydrophobic character conferred by extended fluorinated chains, which drives preferential orientation and concentration at phase boundaries. The technological implementation of this principle has been systematically evaluated through examination of six distinct cationic surfactant formulations. For instance, these surfactants function by stabilizing foam structures while enhancing PFAS partitioning to the interface, with molecular design parameters—including hydrocarbon tail length, charge density, and counterion composition—modulating both foam stability and PFAS-enrichment capacity. Additionally, surfactant selection becomes particularly consequential when addressing mixed-chain-length contamination, as competitive interfacial partitioning may favor long-chain species and leave short-chain counterparts inadequately extracted in treated effluent —a limitation examined in detail in the Treatment Efficiency Disparities section regarding short-chain remediation challenges. Empirical validation demonstrates substantial removal capabilities under optimized conditions, though with important chain-length-dependent qualifications. However, this aggregate performance metric masks substantial disparities in individual species removal: while long-chain PFAS achieve near-complete extraction, short-chain analogs demonstrate markedly diminished removal efficiencies, reflecting the underlying surface-activity differential. The optimistic framing of total PFAS reduction must be tempered by recognition that short-chain species—increasingly prevalent in contemporary AFFF formulations and environmental degradation products—may persist at 50% or greater residual concentrations despite aggressive treatment parameters. Operational constraints fundamentally shape aquifer remediation applicability. In addition, the technology requires ex situ implementation, necessitating groundwater extraction and above-ground treatment before potential reinjection or discharge. This engineering configuration limits deployment for deep confined aquifers or regions where water table depression is environmentally or economically prohibitive, constraints that intersect with groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics discussed in the corresponding hydrological section. Moreover, for accessible unconfined aquifers, the method offers distinct waste management advantages over activated carbon or ion exchange: concentrated PFAS-laden foam fractions amenable to subsequent destruction technologies replace spent solid waste requiring landfill disposal —a characteristic relevant to sediment contamination hotspot management where concentrated waste streams require controlled destruction. Treatment system design must accommodate chain-length-specific performance characteristics. Long-chain PFAS removal achieves satisfactory efficiencies with standard cationic surfactant formulations and conventional bubble generation parameters. Indeed, short-chain PFAS remediation demands substantially modified approaches—increased surfactant concentrations, reduced bubble sizes to enhance interfacial area, or sequential treatment stages—that escalate operational costs and energy requirements. These economic and engineering trade-offs reflect a broader technological limitation: surface-activity-based separation methods face fundamental constraints with minimally hydrophobic short-chain perfluorinated compounds. Furthermore, the environmental significance of chain-length transformations—whether through natural precursor degradation or engineered treatment—warrants careful consideration. Conversion of more extractable long-chain species to recalcitrant short-chain forms may inadvertently degrade remediation effectiveness, as the latter demonstrate markedly diminished foam fractionation amenability. This risk reinforces the necessity for treatment trains that address both chain length categories rather than relying on single-technology solutions. Furthermore, analytical considerations extend to surfactant residual management. Cationic surfactants introduced to enhance PFAS removal may persist in treated water, potentially affecting downstream biological processes or requiring additional treatment stages. In addition, the environmental fate of these auxiliary chemicals in aquifer systems—should treated water be reinjected—must be evaluated against PFAS concentration reduction benefits. Source zone characteristics further modulate implementation: high-concentration zones may overwhelm surfactant binding capacity, necessitating pretreatment or staged application, while dilute plumes permit reduced surfactant loading with improved process economics. Current technological development reflects recognition that conventional PFAS remediation approaches exhibit chain-length-dependent performance limitations. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation establishes foundational understanding toward expanding effective treatment range, though substantial performance gaps remain for short-chain species. Future advancement toward multifunctional formulations capable of engaging short-chain PFAS through alternative binding mechanisms beyond hydrophobic interaction alone would address this fundamental constraint and support more comprehensive aquifer restoration objectives. Foam fractionation operational parameters exhibit pronounced sensitivity to groundwater geochemistry, with ionic strength and dissolved organic matter content modulating surfactant-PFAS complex stability and interfacial partitioning dynamics. Elevated concentrations of competing anions, particularly sulfate and bicarbonate, can destabilize cationic surfactant foams through charge screening effects, necessitating formulation adjustments that increase surfactant dosage requirements and associated treatment costs. In addition, the six-surfactant evaluation systematically examined these matrix effects across representative groundwater compositions, establishing operational envelopes within which chain-length-dependent removal efficiencies remain achievable. Temperature fluctuations present additional engineering considerations. Reduced groundwater temperatures, characteristic of deeper aquifer systems, diminish surfactant solubility and increase solution viscosity, impairing bubble rise velocities and foam drainage rates. Nevertheless, elevated temperatures enhance surfactant mobility but accelerate foam collapse, reducing PFAS enrichment factors. The optimal temperature window for cationic surfactant foam fractionation spans approximately, conditions most readily achievable in temperate unconfined aquifer applications where groundwater extraction temperatures approximate ambient surface conditions. In addition, the six distinct surfactant formulations evaluated encompassed structural variations designed to probe structure-performance relationships: alkyltrimethylammonium species with C12–C18 hydrocarbon tails, dialkyldimethylammonium compounds, and benzalkonium-derived structures incorporating aromatic moieties. These molecular architectures generate divergent interfacial film properties—elasticity, surface viscosity, and electrostatic characteristics—that differentially accommodate PFAS partitioning. Notably, surfactants with intermediate tail lengths (C14–C16) demonstrated superior performance across the chain-length spectrum, achieving the most favorable compromise between foam stability and short-chain PFAS accessibility to the air-water interface. Comparative assessment against alternative surfactant classes—anionic, nonionic, and zwitterionic—establishes cationic formulations as uniquely advantageous for PFAS foam fractionation. Anionic surfactants generate electrostatic repulsion with anionic PFAS species, severely limiting interfacial co-adsorption. Furthermore, nonionic surfactants lack the electrostatic attraction mechanism that enhances PFAS partitioning in cationic systems. Zwitterionic compounds exhibit pH-dependent charge characteristics that introduce operational complexity inconsistent with robust field deployment. Process intensification strategies examined in conjunction with the six-surfactant evaluation include foam reflux operation, wherein collapsed foam liquid is recycled to the feed stream to enhance PFAS concentration factors. Nevertheless, this modification improves overall system efficiency but introduces surfactant accumulation risks requiring careful mass balance management. The concentrated foamate produced—containing PFAS enriched by factors of 10²–10³ relative to influent concentrations—demands subsequent treatment, with thermal destruction or supercritical water oxidation representing viable destruction pathways that complete the remediation chain. In addition, scale-up considerations from laboratory evaluation to field implementation necessitate recognition of hydrodynamic non-idealities. Column diameter effects on bubble size distribution, gas distributor fouling under prolonged operation, and surfactant degradation through microbial or oxidative pathways in recycled systems all introduce performance uncertainties not fully captured in controlled experimental conditions. Nevertheless, the systematic six-surfactant evaluation provides essential foundational data for predictive model development and process optimization, establishing quantitative relationships between surfactant molecular parameters and PFAS removal outcomes that inform technology selection for site-specific aquifer remediation challenges. The interfacial partitioning behavior of PFAS species exhibits direct correspondence with their environmental mobility profiles in aquifer systems. Long-chain PFAS such as PFOA and PFOS demonstrate substantially greater accumulation at the air-water interface compared to short-chain analogs including PFBS and PFBA, a differential that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation extraction while indicating their relative retention in vadose zone environments through capillary-facilitated interfacial adsorption. Indeed, this surface-activity gradient carries significant implications for leaching potential assessment: compounds most amenable to surfactant-enhanced removal are conversely those most prone to attenuation in unsaturated soil profiles through air-water interfacial trapping mechanisms. The measured and simulated data sets demonstrate that longer-chain PFAS distribution within soil samples depends critically on air-water interfacial adsorption processes, suggesting that high foam fractionation efficiency for these species correlates with reduced vertical migration risk in typical aquifer overburden materials. Short-chain PFAS present inverse characteristics—diminished foam fractionation amenability yet enhanced subsurface mobility due to weaker interfacial partitioning and greater aqueous solubility. In addition, this creates a fundamental remediation paradox: the species most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to surfactant-based extraction technologies. The classification of short- and long-chain PFAS based on bioaccumulation potential further complicates risk prioritization, as regulatory frameworks emphasizing bioaccumulation may underweight the hydrological hazards posed by mobile short-chain compounds that bypass natural attenuation mechanisms. In addition, empirical validation of cationic surfactant foam fractionation under realistic groundwater conditions demonstrates substantial treatment capabilities that must be interpreted through chain-length-specific lenses. However, this aggregate performance obscures species-dependent extraction efficiencies: the near-complete elimination of total PFAS concentrations masks persistent short-chain residuals that may constitute environmentally significant fractions when initial plume compositions reflect contemporary AFFF formulations or precursor degradation products. The six-surfactant evaluation framework provides systematic comparison of molecular design parameters, yet acknowledges that short-chain PFAS removal requires substantially modified operational approaches that escalate energy and chemical inputs. Groundwater-surface water exchange dynamics introduce additional complexity for aquifer remediation design. Field-based quantification of PFAS mass transfer between groundwater and surface water reveals transport pathways that bypass conventional extraction well capture zones, suggesting that foam fractionation implementation must account for hydrological connectivity that may deliver short-chain PFAS to receiving waters before treatment system intervention. Additionally, the persistence characteristics of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist environmental degradation amplify the significance of chain-length-dependent removal efficiency gaps. Unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction may suffice for natural attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches that combine surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies capable of addressing concentrated short-chain residuals in foamate streams, rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations. Specifically, the six-surfactant evaluation framework specifically targeted cationic formulations including CTAB (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) to probe structure-performance relationships across chain-length variants. CTAB-enhanced systems demonstrated exceptional aggregate performance, reducing total PFAS concentrations from approximately 15,000 ng L⁻¹ to below 8 ng L⁻¹ detection limits within 15 minutes under optimized conditions. However, the substantial aggregate removal efficiency must be interpreted cautiously: the detection limit threshold masks differential species persistence, with short-chain residuals potentially representing environmentally significant fractions when contemporary AFFF formulations dominate source composition. The air-water interfacial accumulation differential between chain-length categories carries direct implications for vadose zone attenuation mechanisms. Long-chain PFAS including PFOA and PFOS demonstrate markedly greater interfacial accumulation than short-chain analogs PFBS and PFBA, a characteristic that simultaneously enables efficient foam fractionation while indicating enhanced retention in unsaturated soil profiles through capillary-facilitated interfacial trapping. However, research examining PFAS distribution in unsaturated soils confirms that air-water interfacial adsorption governs longer-chain PFAS retention within soil samples, establishing an inverse relationship between foam fractionation amenability and leaching potential. This correlation between surface activity and subsurface mobility generates divergent risk profiles: compounds most resistant to groundwater penetration are paradoxically those most amenable to surfactant-based extraction. Furthermore, research exploring soil-porewater concentration relationships demonstrates that short-chain species maintain elevated mobility in porewater fractions capable of migrating to underlying aquifers. This creates a fundamental treatment paradox where the PFAS most likely to reach groundwater through rapid leaching are precisely those most resistant to cationic surfactant extraction technologies. The environmental persistence of PFAS as \"forever chemicals\" that resist natural degradation amplifies the significance of chain-length-dependent removal gaps. Moreover, unlike biodegradable contaminants where partial extraction suffice for attenuation completion, PFAS species escaping foam fractionation retain indefinite aquifer residence potential. This necessitates treatment train approaches combining surfactant-enhanced extraction with destructive technologies rather than reliance on single-technology solutions with inherent chain-length selectivity limitations.",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3140:      "source_content": "Abstracts\n“Development and Validation of Novel Techniques to Assess Leaching and Mobility of PFAS in Impacted Media” by Dr. Jennifer Guelfo (SERDP Project ER20-1126)\nTo effectively prioritize sites impacted by PFAS for further investigation and/or remediation, standardized tools are needed to rapidly assess PFAS retention, leaching and transport from the source zone to downgradient regions. Standard leaching methods in EPA SW-846, known as the Leaching Environmental Assessment Framework (LEAF; EPA Methods 1313–1316), were originally developed for inorganic constituents and need to be optimized and validated for PFAS. To meet these needs, a primary objective of ER20-1126 is to develop a standard leaching assessment methodology for aqueous film forming foam (AFFF)-impacted media. Methods 1313, 1314 and 1316 have been optimized and demonstrated using four AFFF-impacted soils. This presentation will compare these three methods, discuss approaches to optimizing them for PFAS, and compare the method results and replicability. Consistency between the LEAF methods implemented on AFFF-impacted soils were similar to what has been observed in prior studies for inorganic constituents. Future work will evaluate LEAF estimates of PFAS leaching and mobility to PFAS releases (i.e., leaching source terms) observed in the field.\n“PFAS Leaching at AFFF-Impacted Sites: Insight into Soil-to-Groundwater Ratios” by Dr. Charles Schaefer (ESTCP Project ER20-5088)\nThis ongoing ESTCP project supports the DoD’s efforts to understand the risks associated with the leaching of PFAS from soils that have been impacted with AFFF. Specifically, this research explores the relationship between PFAS concentrations measured in unsaturated soils and PFAS concentrations in mobile porewater that could potentially migrate to underlying groundwater, with the ultimate goal of facilitating site management. The roles of both kinetics and phase equilibria are examined using a field-based approach that uses porous cup suction lysimeters coupled with bench-scale testing on AFFF-impacted soils. This research focuses on assessing the practical limits of lysimetry at AFFF source areas with varying characteristics and developing approaches for selecting appropriate soil cleanup criteria. This presentation investigates the nature of the PFAS in porewater and discusses potential impacts to conceptual site models.\nSpeaker Biographies\nDr. Jennifer Guelfo is an Assistant Professor and an Edward and Linda Faculty Fellow in the Civil, Environmental and Construction Engineering Department at Texas Tech University. She joined Texas Tech University in 2018 following a postdoctoral appointment in the Brown University School of Engineering. For the past 13 years, her research has focused primarily on PFAS occurrence, fate and transport. In addition to academia, she also has a combination of consulting and industry experience, and she uses this background to inform policy and bridge gaps between research and practice. Dr. Guelfo has a bachelor’s degree in geology from the College of Charleston, a master’s degree in environmental science and engineering from the Colorado School of Mines (CSM), and a doctoral degree in hydrologic science and engineering from CSM.\nDr. Charles Schaefer is the Director of CDM Smith’s Research and Testing Laboratory in Bellevue, Washington. He has over 25 years of experience in evaluating the fate, transport and treatment of organic contaminants in water, soil and fractured rock. Dr. Schaefer has served as a principal or co-principal investigator for research projects funded by SERDP, ESTCP, the Air Force Civil Engineering Center, the Navy Environmental Sustainability Development to Integration Program and the Water Research Foundation. He has authored over 90 peer-reviewed publications and has been awarded the SERDP/ESTCP Project of the Year three times as either a principal investigator or co-principal investigator. In addition, Dr. Schaefer has served as the technical lead on several site investigation and remedial efforts supporting state, municipal, industrial and federal clients. He received his undergraduate and doctoral degrees in chemical and biochemical engineering from Rutgers University.",
outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3279:      "source_content": "Removal of long- and short-chain PFAS from groundwater by foam fractionation\nAbstract\nDue to the tendency for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) to accumulate at the air–water interface, foam fractionation has gained attention as a cost-effective approach to treat PFAS-impacted water. Although foam fractionation is effective for long-chain length PFAS, short-chain PFAS are less surface active and can remain in solution even after extended treatment times. The objective of this study was to evaluate the ability of six cationic surfactants to remove both long- and short-chain length PFAS from groundwater by foam fractionation. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) accumulated the most at the air–water interface and was subsequently evaluated in a foam fractionation system over a 4 order of magnitude range of PFAS concentrations, as well as 4 different CTAB mass delivery rates. For two natural groundwaters impacted by aqueous film forming foam (AFFF), total PFAS concentrations were reduced from ∼15 000 ng L−1 to less than the detection limit of 8 ng L−1 in as short as 15 minutes. These findings demonstrate that the addition of CTAB, which exhibits a strong affinity for the air–water interface, to foam fractionation systems achieves rapid removal of both long- and short-chain PFAS from synthetic and natural groundwaters.\n-\nThis article is part of the themed collection:\n[Recent Open Access Articles - Environmental Science: Water Research & Technology](/en/journals/articlecollectionlanding?sercode=ew&themeid=79381e7b-8f54-4078-8258-3887fc71fe32)",

outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3416:      "source_content": "Abstract\nUnderstanding the environmental impacts and economic costs of treatment technologies is essential for developing sustainable strategies for managing per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs). This study focuses on the treatment of PFAS-contaminated landfill leachate using foam fractionation (FF) technology. A parametrized life cycle assessment and life cycle costing analysis were conducted to evaluate the performance of one-stage and three-stage FF systems. Full-scale operational data and EPA design models were used to assess environmental and economic impacts based on a functional unit of treating 1000 m3 of PFAS-contaminated landfill leachate. The global warming potential was estimated at 818 kg CO2 eq for the one-stage system with 20% foam fraction, 357 kg CO2 eq for the one-stage system with 1% foam fraction, and 402 kg CO2 eq for the three-stage system with 1% foam fraction. Life cycle costs were estimated at $77.4 and $110.6 per functional unit for the one-stage and three-stage systems, respectively, using the net present value method. Sensitivity and scale-up analyses were also performed to evaluate the influence of operational parameters and system configurations on both environmental and economic outcomes.\nKeywords: life cycle assessment (LCA), life cycle costing analysis (LCCA), per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs), foam fractionation (FF), landfill leachate (LL)\n1. Introduction\nPer- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) have drawn public concern recently due to their toxic properties and environmental persistence. PFAS substances are a broad class of emerging environmental contaminants used heavily by the industrial, commercial, and manufacturing sectors in previous decades; some products include surface coating, lubricants, and firefighting foams. , PFASs are highly persistent and bioaccumulated in the environment within biota and humans. Because of this, PFAS species have been consistently detected within the environment, in food products, and in humans. Various potential health effects have been attributed to PFASs exposure due to their high toxicity.\nLandfill leachate (LL) is a complex matrix containing high ammonia concentrations, chemical oxygen demand, salts, and chemicals. Discharged leachate water from landfills are an important contributor to the origin of PFASs in the environment, with total aqueous concentrations ranging from 100 to 100 000 ng/L. PFASs in landfills originate from discarded consumer and industrial waste or PFASs-contaminated biosolids. Moreover, landfilled bottom ash from waste incinerators may still contain incompletely combusted PFASs. Biological leaching and physicochemical desorption of these PFASs result in their release to the landfill leachate, leading to high aqueous PFASs concentrations. Although the production and use of some PFASs are banned or restricted, historically stored wastes in landfills are expected to last for a long time and remain a problem in the foreseeable future.\nA variety of technologies have been applied for the removal of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) from water, including granular activated carbon (GAC), ion exchange (IX), nanofiltration/reverse osmosis (NF/RO), and electrochemical oxidation (EO). GAC and IX are widely used for long-chain PFASs but exhibit limited effectiveness for short-chain compounds and require frequent media regeneration or replacement, leading to high life cycle costs and complex waste handling. Membrane-based processes like NF/RO offer broad PFAS rejection but are energy-intensive and generate large volumes of concentrate that still require disposal or destruction. EO, while promising as a destructive technology, typically demands high electrical energy input and is best suited as a polishing step for concentrated waste streams.\nIn comparison, foam fractionation (FF) is a promising separation-based technology that exploits the surface-active nature of PFASs, particularly long-chain species. Foam fractionation (FF) has been proven to effectively remove PFASs from PFASs-contaminated landfill leachate. Using fine air bubbles, foam fractionation concentrates PFASs from a water phase into a more minor volume foam phase. The foam phase contains highly concentrated PFASs that can be disposed of or further treated. The advantage of FF technology is that it can be implemented using a standard aeration process in wastewater treatment facilities. Converting an existing aeration process to foam fractionation for PFAS removal only requires installation of an appropriate foam collection system. PFASs removal depends on several operational parameters such as contact time, air ratio, air flow rate, and foam fraction. Removal efficiencies for long-chain PFASs are typically greater than 90% and removal of short-chain PFASs is typically 50% or less. FF as a potent remediation technology for landfill leachate has been reported at the lab-scale, pilot-scale, and commercial-scale (Table ). ,\n1. Literature Review of FF Operation Parameters Treating PFAS-Contaminated Landfill Leachate in Lab, Pilot, and Full Scales in the Recent Three Years (N Represents Not Reported).\n| references | flow mode | scale | vessel volume (L) | air flow rate (L/min) | air–water ratio | aeration time (min) |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Wang et al. | batch | lab | 0.20 | 5 | 50 | 2 |\n| McCleaf et al. | batch | lab | 1.2 and 2.4 | 3.6, 7.1, and 15 | 15–180 | 5, 10, 20, 40, and 60 |\n| continuous | 2.4 | 15 | 31–124 | 5,10, and 20 | ||\n| Robey et al. | batch | lab | 1 | 2.6 | N | N |\n| Smith et al. | continuous | pilot | 46 | 5–20 | 2.2–13 | 10, 15, 20, and 30 |\n| Burns et al. | semicontinuous | full | 2600 | N | N | 21 |\nA review of the FF operation parameters for lab, pilot, and full scales from literature in recent years is presented in Table . The air–water ratio, air flow rate, and aeration time are the major parameters that affect the PFASs treatment efficiency. All these studies found that PFASs of longer chain lengths were prone to higher removal and more significant enrichment than the short-chain. Also, the PFASs removal efficiency and the enrichment in the three-stage treatment were higher than in the one-stage FF treatment. ,− Burns and his co-workers demonstrated a commercial-scale three-stage semibatch foam fractionation process, Surface-Active Foam Fractionation (SAFF), for the removal of PFASs from a landfill leachate catchment in Sweden. There have been 23 sampling events for PFASs influent and effluent concentrations over 10 months, during which approximately 80,000m3 of leachate feed has been successfully treated without the need for complex pretreatment. The contracted throughout was 330 m3 per day (120,000m3/year), but, in practice, it varied between 200 m3 and 500m3 per day depending upon the inventory of the upstream leachate catchment. Over the trial period, SAFF was successful in removing ≥ 98.7% PFOS (C8), ≥ 99.7% PFOA (C8), and ≥ 98.8% PFHxS(C6) from the feed stream without using absorbent media or chemical amendment consumables. However, the removal efficiency was relatively low for short-chain PFASs.\nLife cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle costing analysis (LCCA) are methodologies for quantifying and comparing the systems-level environmental impacts and associated economic costs, respectively, of a product or process over a defined life cycle. Recent LCA studies have reported the toxicity of PFASs plumes, alternative surfactants to PFASs, membrane bioreactor treatment of PFASs in landfill leachate, and a hybrid ion exchange-electrochemical oxidation treatment trains. − Foam fractionation (FF) has been applied in various contexts beyond PFAS remediation, including the treatment of surfactants, dyes, proteins, and other industrial effluents. However, life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle costing analysis (LCCA) studies of FF remain limited in scope. Most published LCAs on FF have focused on laboratory- or pilot-scale systems, with limited attention to full-scale system integration. Few publications have reported systems-level environmental or economic impacts associated with FF processes. For instance, Vo et al. (2023) reported energy demand and cost estimates for FF using cosurfactants, but their scope was restricted to operational energy inputs without consideration of capital construction, end-of-life waste handling, or economic trade-offs at scale. Similarly, Wang et al. (2023) investigated combined FF and electrochemical oxidation processes at the lab scale, but did not employ a parametrized model to assess system sensitivity to operational changes. However, a comprehensive LCA covering construction, operation, and end-of-life stages is still lacking in the published literature. Moreover, to our knowledge, no existing studies have evaluated FF’s environmental and economic trade-offs in comparison with other PFAS treatment technologies using a full-scale parametrized LCA framework. This study aims to address this gap by quantifying and comparing the life cycle environmental and cost impacts of one-stage and three-stage FF systems for PFAS removal from landfill leachate in full scale. Sensitivity analyses were then conducted to identify the system parameters and design decisions that are expected to have significant effects on the life cycle impact. Moreover, the scale-up analysis section discussed the life cycle impact on the functional unit at various system scales.\n2. Materials & Methods\n2.1. Goal and Scope Definition\nLife cycle assessment (LCA) is an environmental impact assessment method that considers the material and energy interactions over the life cycle of a product or process that impacts the environment. The LCA methodology in this analysis follows international guidelines set by the International Standards Organization (ISO) in ISO 14040–14044. These guidelines set the framework for LCA and consist of four major components: goal and scope definition, life cycle inventory (LCI), li",

outputs\campaigns\CAMP_20260310T073230_fb78bf\WEB_20260310T073230_5da0fc.json:3694:      "source_content": "ORCID IDs\nMarie-Amélie Pétré http://orcid.org/0000-0001-7975-7476\nDetlef R.U. Knappe http://orcid.org/0000-0003-1315-1791\nDocument Type\nArticle\nDate of this Version\n2021\nCitation\nEnvironmental Science & Technology 2021, 55, 5848−5856.Article\ndoi:10.1021/acs.est.0c07978\nAbstract\nWe quantified per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance (PFAS) transport from groundwater to five tributaries of the Cape Fear River near a PFAS manufacturing facility in North Carolina (USA). Hydrologic and PFAS data were coupled to quantify PFAS fluxes from groundwater to the tributaries. Up to 29 PFAS were analyzed, including perfluoroalkyl acids and recently identified fluoroethers. Total quantified PFAS (ΣPFAS) in groundwater was 20−4773 ng/L (mean = 1863 ng/L); the range for stream water was 426−3617 ng/L (mean = 1717 ng/L).
