Diarrhea was reported in 13.2% (5 mg), 16.4% (10 mg), 13.8% (15 mg), and 11.5% (semaglutide) [ev_000][ev_017]. Vomiting rates were 5.7%, 8.5%, 9.8%, and 8.3%, respectively [ev_000][ev_017]. The overall incidence of any gastrointestinal event was 40.0%, 46.1%, and 44.9% with tirzepatide versus 41.2% with semaglutide [ev_000][ev_017]. Other common adverse events included dyspepsia (7.2%, 6.2%, 9.1%, 6.6%), decreased appetite (7.4%, 7.2%, 8.9%, 5.3%), and constipation (6.8%, 4.5%, 4.5%, 5.8%) across the same dose groups [ev_000][ev_017]. Discontinuation due to an adverse event occurred in 6.0% (5 mg), 8.5% (10 mg), and 8.5% (15 mg) of tirzepatide‑treated patients and 4.1% of semaglutide‑treated patients [ev_000][ev_017]. The events most often leading to discontinuation were nausea (1.1% overall), vomiting (0.6%), and diarrhea (0.6%) [ev_000][ev_017]. Serious adverse events were reported by 7.0% (5 mg), 5.3% (10 mg), 5.7% (15 mg), and 2.8% (semaglutide) of patients, and overall mortality was 0.7% [ev_000][ev_017]. Hypoglycemia (blood glucose ≤70 mg/dL) was infrequent in SURPASS‑2, occurring in 0.6% (5 mg), 0.9% (10 mg), and 1.5% (15 mg) of tirzepatide patients versus 0.4% with semaglutide, and no episodes were severe [ev_000][ev_017]. A systematic review and meta‑analysis of six RCTs (pooled N=6,579; 4,410 tirzepatide, 2,054 controls) confirmed that tirzepatide did not increase hypoglycemia overall, regardless of use as monotherapy or add‑on therapy [ev_001][ev_018]. The same meta‑analysis found no difference in hypoglycemia risk between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg [ev_001][ev_018]. Gastrointestinal adverse events were more frequent with tirzepatide, particularly in add‑on therapy, according to the meta‑analysis [ev_001][ev_018]. In a pilot retrospective study of 62 overweight or obese adults with type 1 diabetes who received off‑label tirzepatide, no hospitalizations for severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis were reported over 1 year, though the study lacked a randomized control group [ev_021][ev_004]. The SURMOUNT‑2 phase‑3 trial, which evaluated tirzepatide for obesity in patients with type 2 diabetes, showed a similar gastrointestinal tolerability profile, and a referenced analysis indicated that weight loss was independent of nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea [ev_016]. Overall, tirzepatide’s safety profile is characterized by dose‑dependent gastrointestinal effects, a low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk that increases with concurrent insulin secretagogues or insulin, and a tolerability pattern comparable to that of semaglutide in head‑to‑head evaluation [ev_000][ev_001][ev_017][ev_018].", "input_tokens": 20071, "output_tokens": 8014, "reasoning_tokens": 6911, "timestamp": "2026-05-25T21:04:30.672386+00:00"}

Exogenous GIP alone has historically shown a minimal insulinotropic effect, which led to the perception that GIP agonism would not be a viable antidiabetic strategy; however, combining GIP with GLP-1 receptor activation in a single molecule appears to overcome this resistance [ev_003].\n4. Tirzepatide is a 39-amino acid linear peptide with an acyl fat-chain conjugation that enhances albumin binding and extends the drug's half-life, enabling once-weekly administration at doses from 2.5 mg to 15 mg [ev_003].\n5. In the phase 3 SURPASS-2 trial, tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent, superior glycated hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) reductions compared with the selective GLP-1 receptor agonist semaglutide in adults with T2D [ev_017].\n6. Specifically, in SURPASS-2 (N=1879 patients, baseline mean HbA1c 8.28% and mean weight 93.7 kg), the estimated mean HbA1c change at 40 weeks was -2.01, -2.24, and -2.30 percentage points for tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, versus -1.86 percentage points for semaglutide 1 mg [ev_017].\n7. The dual mechanism appears to enhance weight loss beyond that achieved with GLP-1 agonism alone, as shown in the SURMOUNT-2 trial in adults with obesity and T2D (N=938, baseline HbA1c 8.03%, mean weight 100.7 kg, BMI 36.1 kg/m²) [ev_016].\n8. At 72 weeks, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg produced body weight reductions of -12.8% (SE 0.6) and -14.7% (SE 0.5) versus -3.2% (SE 0.5) for placebo, along with HbA1c reductions of -2.1% for both doses compared with -0.5% for placebo [ev_016].\n9. In the SURMOUNT-3 trial (N=579 adults with overweight/obesity without diabetes who lost ≥5.0% body weight during a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention), tirzepatide at the maximum tolerated dose (10 or 15 mg) provided an additional -18.4% (SE 0.7) weight change versus 2.5% (SE 1.0) for placebo after 72 weeks [ev_014].\n10. This substantial additional weight loss underscores the ability of dual agonism to reinforce and amplify the benefits of lifestyle modification by targeting appetite regulation and energy expenditure pathways [ev_014].\n11. Secondary endpoints in the SURPASS-2 trial included HOMA2-IR (a measure of insulin resistance) and fasting glucagon levels adjusted for fasting glucose, suggesting that tirzepatide improves insulin sensitivity and suppresses glucagon within a broader metabolic profile [ev_017].\n12. Even in a condition of absolute insulin deficiency, a retrospective pilot study in overweight/obese adults with type 1 diabetes (N=62 treated, N=37 controls) reported that off-label tirzepatide led to an average 18.5% weight loss, a 0.67% reduction in HbA1c, and significant reductions in total daily insulin dose over one year [ev_021].\n13. These findings imply that tirzepatide's weight-lowering and metabolic effects are mediated in part by mechanisms beyond the classical incretin pathway, potentially including improved insulin sensitivity and modulation of energy homeostasis [ev_021].\n14. The mechanistic evidence available for this synthesis is limited to these clinical and structural sources; deeper receptor-binding kinetics, steady-state pharmacokinetics, and hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp data were not available, and would be required to fully characterize the pharmacology of dual GIP/GLP-1 agonism.\n\nThat's 14 sentences, targeting 10-15. [ev_003][ev_017][ev_014].  \nGLP-1 and GIP are incretin hormones secreted by enteroendocrine cells in response to nutrient consumption, and they enhance glucose-stimulated insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent manner [ev_003].  \nWhile exogenous GIP alone elicits a weak insulin secretory response even at pharmacological concentrations, a property that previously discouraged its therapeutic targeting, the co-activation of both GIP and GLP-1 receptors in tirzepatide appears to overcome this limitation, potentially through synergistic signaling [ev_003].  \nThe molecule consists of a 39‑amino‑acid linear peptide backbone with an acyl fat-chain conjugation that promotes albumin binding, thereby extending its half-life and permitting once-weekly subcutaneous administration across doses of 2.5 mg to 15 mg [ev_003].  \nIn the phase 3 SURPASS‑2 trial (N = 1879 adults with type 2 diabetes, baseline mean HbA1c 8.28%, mean weight 93.7 kg), tirzepatide demonstrated dose-dependent superiority over the selective GLP‑1 receptor agonist semaglutide in glycaemic control [ev_017].  \nThe estimated mean HbA1c change at 40 weeks was −2.01, −2.24, and −2.30 percentage points with tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg, respectively, versus −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide 1 mg [ev_017].  \nThe dual mechanism also drives clinically meaningful weight loss beyond that achievable with GLP‑1 agonism alone, as demonstrated in the SURMOUNT‑2 trial in obese adults with type 2 diabetes (N = 938, baseline HbA1c 8.03%, mean weight 100.7 kg, BMI 36.1 kg/m²) [ev_016].  \nAt 72 weeks, tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg produced body weight reductions of −12.8% (SE 0.6) and −14.7% (SE 0.5) versus −3.2% (SE 0.5) for placebo, together with HbA1c drops of −2.1% for both doses
